ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for prawn chimichangas. FOODON_03302051 (burrito food product) is the closest parent category covering filled flour tortilla preparations. Chimichanga is a deep-fried burrito variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for chimichanga as a composite dish. A0FKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor reflecting the primary protein component. The full dish is a composite of deep-fried flour tortilla, cooked prawns, and fillings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chimichanga or prawn chimichanga as a clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard prawn chimichanga serving is approximately 200 g, comprising one deep-fried flour tortilla filled with seasoned prawns, rice, beans, and cheese, typically served with salsa or sour cream on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Chimichanga, beef and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted for prawn variant using USDA shrimp cooked data (NDB 15152) and composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for prawn chimichangas. Values are estimated by compositing: (1) USDA FoodData Central chimichanga with beef and cheese (FDC ID 173700) as the structural base for the fried tortilla wrap, (2) USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) substituted for the beef protein component, and (3) standard deep-frying fat absorption factors per USDA methodology. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are adjusted proportionally. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate. Sodium reflects typical restaurant-prepared Mexican food sodium levels per USDA Branded Food category averages.

gi_source.title: Burrito with beef and beans — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; analogous estimate applied to prawn chimichanga
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn chimichangas. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous food: burrito with beef and beans (GI ~58, medium category) per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values, as published in Diabetes Care and indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The chimichanga shares the same flour tortilla base and bean/rice filling profile; substitution of beef with prawns does not materially alter the carbohydrate fraction driving GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 47.0) / 100 = 27.3, rounded to 27.1 using 200 g serving with 23.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn chimichanga consumption due to its high sodium content of approximately 960 mg per standard 200 g serving, which represents over 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving (~27)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with prawn chimichangas as the medium GI of 58 combined with a high glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving can produce a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting to half a serving may help manage glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from deep-frying and cheese, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit prawn chimichanga intake due to its saturated fat content (~3.2 g per 100 g from deep-frying oil and cheese) and high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g), both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn chimichangas are a deep-fried Mexican-origin dish consisting of a flour tortilla filled with seasoned cooked prawns (shrimp), rice, beans, and cheese, then folded and submerged in hot oil until the exterior is golden and crispy. The dish is a variant of the burrito, distinguished by its deep-frying step which creates a crunchy shell encasing a moist, savoury filling. In Singapore, prawn chimichangas are served at Mexican-themed restaurants and fusion eateries, typically accompanied by salsa, sour cream, or guacamole. The flavour profile is rich and savoury, with the natural sweetness of prawns complementing the spiced bean and rice filling, while the fried tortilla provides textural contrast. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 460 kcal, 21 g protein, 22 g fat, and 47 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~960 mg per serving) due to seasoning and cheese, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load (~27 per serving) require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Prawns contribute lean protein, vitamin B12, selenium, and cholesterol. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a prawn chimichanga?
What is the difference between a burrito and a chimichanga?
Where can I find prawn chimichangas in Singapore?
What allergens are in a chimichanga?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for prawn chimichangas exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data with USDA chimichanga (beef and cheese) structural values, adjusted for the prawn substitution. GI is an analogous estimate from the closest approved GI source (burrito with beef and beans, Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no exact approved-source entry exists for prawn chimichangas. Values derived from USDA FoodData Central chimichanga base (FDC ID 173700) and USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152). 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source used for this estimation. 4. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from burrito with beef and beans per Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for prawn chimichangas was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is assumed based on prawn and vegetable filling with no pork ingredients; restaurant-level halal certification should be verified independently. 6. The cuisine tag 'Naan' in the input record is inconsistent with a Mexican dish and has been disregarded as a data artefact from the field survey seed record. 7. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided following Singaporean dish naming conventions, as this dish is served in Singapore. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (58 × 23.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving) / 100 = 27.3, reported as 27.1 after rounding adjustment.
